documentTitle: A Packet's Journey: TCP/IP Point to Point
lede: When data travels from one host to another, it doesn't travel naked. Each TCP/IP layer wraps it in an "envelope" containing addresses and rules — then at the end of the journey, those envelopes are opened one by one, in exactly the reverse order they were put on. Press the steps below to follow a single packet, from the sending application to the receiving one.

legend.title: Quick reference: what's added & dropped per layer
legend.sub: General rule: moving down (encapsulation) = adding a header. Moving up (decapsulation) = dropping a header. The data unit's name changes at every layer.

rows.added: — (this is where data originates, e.g. an HTTP request)
rows.removed: No header is dropped; data is handed straight to the application

rows.added: Source & destination port, sequence & ack number, flags, window size, checksum (±20 bytes)
rows.removed: TCP header is dropped once the sequence number & checksum are verified; the port is used to route to the destination application

rows.added: Source & destination IP, TTL, protocol, header checksum (±20 bytes)
rows.removed: IP header is dropped once TTL & checksum are checked, confirming the destination is indeed this host

rows.added: Source & destination MAC, EtherType, and an FCS trailer for error detection (±18 bytes)
rows.removed: Header & trailer are dropped at every hop — this is the only layer routers strip and reattach

steps.title: The application creates data
steps.desc: Everything begins with an application on Host A, say a browser building an HTTP request. At this stage the data is still plain — no addresses yet, no delivery rules, just the message content itself.

steps.title: Transport adds a TCP Header → becomes a Segment
steps.desc: The Transport layer wraps the data with a TCP header. This header carries port numbers so it can later be routed to the right application on the receiving end, plus a sequence number so the data can be reassembled and verified as complete.

steps.title: Internet adds an IP Header → becomes a Packet
steps.desc: The Internet layer adds the source and destination IP addresses — this is what lets the data know where to go globally, even across many networks. A TTL is also inserted to stop the packet from looping forever.

steps.title: Link adds an Ethernet Header + Trailer → becomes a Frame
steps.desc: The bottom-most layer adds MAC addresses — physical addresses for the next hop only, not the final destination. An FCS trailer is also appended at the end of the frame to detect if data got corrupted during physical transmission.
fields: physical address of the next hop (Router)
marks the payload as an IP packet
checksum at the end of the frame

steps.title: The frame is sent as bits over the physical medium
steps.desc: The now-complete frame is turned into electrical signals, light, or radio waves and flows along the cable or through the air toward the Router. At this point, the entire wrapper (TCP + IP + Ethernet) travels along with the data.
fields: copper cable / fiber optic / Wi-Fi radio

steps.title: The Router unwraps Ethernet, then reattaches it
steps.desc: The Router only cares about the Link and Internet layers. It strips the old Ethernet header, checks the IP header to know where to forward the packet, decrements the TTL by 1, then attaches a NEW Ethernet header with the MAC address for the next hop.
fields: TTL decremented by 1 in the IP header
new Ethernet header toward Host B
steps.sizeUnit: total bytes unchanged, MAC contents swapped

steps.title: The new frame flows toward Host B
steps.desc: With the new MAC address attached, the frame continues its journey as bits toward Host B. The IP and TCP headers inside are left completely untouched — both are end-to-end and stay intact from the original sender all the way to the final receiver.

steps.title: Host B unwraps the Ethernet Header
steps.desc: Host B receives the frame, verifies the FCS to make sure nothing was corrupted in transit, then drops the Ethernet header and trailer since its job is done: delivering the bits to the right device.
fields: FCS trailer to verify data integrity

steps.title: Host B unwraps the IP Header
steps.desc: The Internet layer checks whether the destination IP address really belongs to this host, verifies the header checksum, then drops the IP header since the job of cross-network addressing is done.
fields: does the destination IP match this host?

steps.title: Host B unwraps the TCP Header — data reaches the application
steps.desc: The Transport layer uses the port number to deliver the data to the right application, verifies the sequence number so the data is assembled correctly, then drops the TCP header. What's left is identical to the original data created by the application on Host A.
fields: destination port → routed to the application
